title: QAssemble: A Pure Python Package for Quantum Many-Body Theory
authors: Seongjun Mo, Dongming Li, Mancheon Han, Johan Jönsson, Byungkyun Kang, Hoonkyung Lee, Gabriel Kotliar, Sangkook Choi
abstract: QAssemble is a pure-Python package for the quantum many-body problem. It implements various functional approaches, such as tight-binding, Hartree-Fock, and GW approximations within a unified object-oriented architecture. Each physical concept--crystal structure, Hamiltonian, Green's function, self-energy, polarizability, screened Coulomb interaction--is represented as a distinct class. The modular design prioritizes code clarity and extensibility, leveraging NumPy, SciPy, and libdlr for numerical operations. Performance-critical kernels, including the polarizability bubble, Dyson equation inversion, and lattice Fourier transforms, are systematically vectorized and combined with the discrete Lehmann representation to achieve practical efficiency within a pure-Python environment. We validate QAssemble on the electronic structure of graphene with local and non-local interactions. Furthermore, benchmarks on a five-orbital extended Hund-Hubbard model demonstrate that this strategy delivers up to a 60x speedup over traditional loop-based Matsubara implementations. QAssemble supports both batch execution for production calculations and interactive workflows for method development.
summary: The authors present QAssemble, a new pure-Python package designed for many-body electronic structure calculations. By replacing traditional Python loops with vectorized array operations and using the Discrete Lehmann Representation, the package achieves significant speedups for GW and Hartree-Fock calculations. The design emphasizes 'hackability' and transparency, making it an ideal tool for researchers prototyping new many-body diagrams. It successfully captures the physics of graphene and provides a scalable foundation for future extensions like DMFT.
main_problem: The difficulty of implementing complex, many-body algorithms (like GW and Hartree-Fock) due to the lack of user-friendly, transparent, and extensible software frameworks that can handle electron correlations in materials.
main_result: The development of QAssemble, a pure-Python package that achieves up to a 60x speedup over traditional loop-based Matsubara implementations by using systematic vectorization and the Discrete Lehmann Representation.
method: An object-oriented, pure-Python approach leveraging NumPy, SciPy, and libdlr, utilizing systematic vectorization of kernels and the Discrete Lehmann Representation (DLR) for frequency-axis compression.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to correlated quantum materials, specifically validated using graphene (two-orbital model) and a five-orbital extended Hund-Hubbard model with local and non-local Coulomb interactions.
key_observables: One-particle Green's functions, self-energy, polarizability, screened Coulomb interaction, density matrix, and spectral functions.
important_parameters: Lattice/orbital structures, Matsubara frequency, imaginary time, temperature, and Coulomb interaction strengths (U, V, J).
main_assumptions: The current implementation is limited to Tight-Binding, Hartree-Fock, and GW approximations; it prioritizes code transparency and extensibility over maximum raw performance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the software architecture and workflow; Figure 2 displays the class hierarchy; Figure 4 compares graphene band structures, density of states, and spectral functions across TB, HF, and GW levels.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific challenge of many-body software, describes the QAssemble object-oriented architecture, details the numerical strategies (vectorization and DLR), validates the physics using graphene, and benchmarks performance using a multi-orbital Hubbard model.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 QAssemble architecture and workflow for many-body electronic structure calculations. Crystal and DLR components define lattice/orbital structures and imaginary- time/Matsubara grids. CorrelationFunction orchestrates tight-binding (TB), HF, and GW methods via fermionic (FLatStc, FLatDyn) and bosonic (BLatStc, BLatDyn) modules
Fig. 2 Core class hierarchy. Class diagram for (a) FLatDyn, (b) FLatStc, (c) BLatDyn, and (d) BLatStc

Figure 3 (a) illustrates the Hartree-Fock self-consistent workflow involving five classes: H0, H, SigH, SigF, and V. The Hamiltonian class acts as the central hub, aggregating self-energy contributions to construct the effective single-particle Hamiltonian at each iteration. The workflow proceeds through the following components.

Fig. 3 Self-consistent workflows for Hartree–Fock and GW approximations. (a) The HF loop iterates between H0, V, SigH, SigF, and Hamiltonian classes to solve H = H0 + ΣH + ΣF self- consistently for the density matrix n. (b) The GW loop extends (a) by additionally computing the irreducible polarizability P = GG (P), the dynamically screened interaction W = V + V PW (W), and the correlation self-energy ΣGW = −GW C (SigGWC), with G combining all contributions via the Dyson equation. Boxes indicate classes; arrows denote data flow between components.

Fig. 4 Electronic structure of graphene. (a) Band structure along high-symmetry paths com- paring TB, HF, and GW results. (b) Density of states obtained by integration over a 20 × 20 × 1 Brillouin zone mesh. (c) A(k, ω) at T = 2000 K along the high-symmetry path Γ-K-M-Γ. The color scale indicates spectral function, with the overlaid black curve showing the quasiparticle band structure obtained from diagonalization of HQP(k) in eq. (3)

Figure 4(b) presents the density of states computed within the three levels of theory, obtained by integrating the spectral function over a 20 × 20 × 1 Brillouin zone mesh. This uniform k-space sampling reveals how electron correlation reshapes the electronic structure across the entire zone, rather than only along high-symmetry paths. The TB DOS exhibits the characteristic semi-metallic behavior of graphene, with a V-shaped dip approaching zero at the Fermi level and symmetric van Hove singularities at ϵ ≈±1 eV arising from saddle points at the M points.
